items.text: Every other authour may aspire to praise; the lexicographer can only hope to escape reproach
items.context: From the Preface to A Dictionary of the English Language, reflecting on the thankless task of dictionary compilation
items.publicReaction: The preface was widely read and established Johnson's authority on language; it became one of the most influential linguistic works of the century
items.source: Samuel Johnson, Preface to A Dictionary of the English Language (London, 1755)

items.text: the English Dictionary was written with little assistance of the learned, and without any patronage of the great; not in the soft obscurities of retirement, or under the shelter of academick bowers, but amidst inconvenience and distraction, in sickness and in sorrow
items.context: From the Dictionary Preface, explaining the difficult circumstances under which he completed his monumental work while his wife was dying
items.publicReaction: Contemporaries recognized both the achievement and Johnson's personal sacrifice; the preface was celebrated for its honesty and eloquence
items.source: Samuel Johnson, Preface to A Dictionary of the English Language (London, 1755)

items.text: Is not a patron...one who looks with unconcern on a man struggling for life in the water, and, when he has reached ground, encumbers him with help?
items.context: Letter to Lord Chesterfield, whom Johnson had sought patronage from years earlier without success; written just as his Dictionary was being published
items.publicReaction: The letter became famous as a statement of independence and a critique of aristocratic patronage; it severely damaged Johnson's relationship with Chesterfield and established Johnson's reputation for dignity
items.source: Samuel Johnson, Letter to the Earl of Chesterfield (7 February 1755)

items.text: I hope not much to tire those I shall not happen to please, and if I am not commended for the beauty of my works, to be at least pardoned for their brevity
items.context: Opening statement of The Rambler, first issue, where Johnson explains his approach to the new periodical essay
items.publicReaction: The essay series became influential despite initial modest sales; the essays were later republished in collected form in 1753 with great success
items.source: Samuel Johnson, The Rambler, No. 1 (20 March 1750)

items.text: no species of writing seems more worthy of cultivation than biography, since none can be more delightful or more useful
items.context: From Rambler No. 60, Johnson's essay on biography as a literary form and moral instruction
items.publicReaction: This essay became influential in establishing the critical importance of biographical writing; Johnson's own preference for intimate biographical detail over grand narratives shaped literary criticism
items.source: Samuel Johnson, The Rambler, No. 60 (13 October 1750)

items.text: more knowledge may be gained of a man's real character, by a short conversation with one of his servants, than from a formal and studied narrative
items.context: From Rambler No. 60, arguing that intimate domestic details reveal character more truthfully than official accounts
items.publicReaction: This statement influenced biographical practice and theory for generations, supporting the value of anecdotal evidence
items.source: Samuel Johnson, The Rambler, No. 60 (13 October 1750)

items.text: Those who attempt periodical essays seem to be often stopped in the beginning, by the difficulty of finding a proper title...Every man is, or hopes to be, an Idler
items.context: Opening of The Idler, No. 1, Johnson's second major essay periodical, where he explains his pen name
items.publicReaction: The Idler essays were well-received and published regularly; Johnson's exploration of idleness as a universal human trait became characteristic of his philosophical approach
items.source: Samuel Johnson, The Idler, No. 1 (15 April 1758)

items.text: Every man in the journey of life takes the same advantage of the ignorance of his fellow travellers, disguises himself in counterfeited merit
items.context: From Adventurer No. 84, Johnson's essay about social pretense, illustrated through the example of stage-coach travelers assuming false personas
items.publicReaction: The observation became widely recognized as a characteristic Johnsonian insight into human vanity and self-deception
items.source: Samuel Johnson, The Adventurer, No. 84 (25 August 1753)

items.text: Sir, a woman's preaching is like a dog's walking on his hinder legs. It is not done well; but you are surprised to find it done at all
items.context: Johnson's witty remark to Boswell after Boswell attended a Quaker meeting where a woman preached
items.publicReaction: The quote became famous as an example of Johnson's satirical wit on gender and propriety; it has been quoted frequently in discussions of Johnson's views on women
items.source: James Boswell, The Life of Samuel Johnson, LL.D. (London, 1791), recording a conversation of 26 July 1763

items.text: No man but a blockhead ever wrote, except for money
items.context: Johnson's blunt statement to Boswell about the economic motives for writing
items.publicReaction: Boswell disagreed with this statement but recorded it as characteristic of Johnson's direct speech; the quote became famous as representing Johnson's pragmatic view of authorship
items.source: James Boswell, The Life of Samuel Johnson, LL.D. (London, 1791), recording a conversation of 5 April 1776

items.text: Why, Sir, you find no man, at all intellectual, who is willing to leave London. No, Sir, when a man is tired of London, he is tired of life; for there is in London all that life can afford
items.context: Johnson's response to Boswell's question about whether his love of London would diminish if he lived there permanently rather than visiting from Scotland
items.publicReaction: This statement became one of Johnson's most famous and frequently quoted remarks about the city; it encapsulates his appreciation for urban society and variety
items.source: James Boswell, The Life of Samuel Johnson, LL.D. (London, 1791), recording a conversation of 20 September 1777

items.text: My dear friend, clear your mind of cant. You may talk as other people do. You may say to a man, 'Sir, I am your most humble servant.' You are not his most humble servant. You may say, 'These are sad times; it is a melancholy thing to be reserved to such times.' You don't mind the times. You tell a man, 'I am sorry you had such bad weather the last day of your journey, and were so much wet.' You don't care six-pence whether he was wet or dry. You may talk in this manner; it is a mode of talking in Society: but don't think foolishly
items.context: Johnson's extended remark to Boswell, cautioning against insincere or sanctimonious speech while acknowledging social conventions
items.publicReaction: This statement became recognized as Johnson's most explicit statement on the distinction between social convention and genuine thought; it has been widely quoted as representing his philosophy
items.source: James Boswell, The Life of Samuel Johnson, LL.D. (London, 1791), Volume 2, pp. 454-455, recording a conversation of 15 May 1783

items.text: There is in this world no real delight (excepting those of sensuality), but exchange of ideas in conversation
items.context: From Johnson's observations on the value of conversation, found in Hester Piozzi's Anecdotes of the Late Samuel Johnson
items.publicReaction: The statement became influential in eighteenth-century philosophy emphasizing social intercourse and intellectual exchange
items.source: Samuel Johnson, quoted in Hester Lynch Piozzi, Anecdotes of the Late Samuel Johnson, LL.D. (London, 1786)

items.text: Questioning is not the mode of conversation among gentlemen. It is assuming a superiority
items.context: Johnson's observation on proper conversational etiquette among educated men
items.publicReaction: This statement influenced eighteenth-century understanding of polite conversation and social hierarchy
items.source: James Boswell, The Life of Samuel Johnson, LL.D. (London, 1791)

items.text: When two Englishmen meet, their first talk is of the weather
items.context: From Johnson's essay The Idler, No. 11, observing the English national habit of discussing meteorological conditions
items.publicReaction: The observation became famous as a characterization of English conversational habits and has been quoted for centuries
items.source: Samuel Johnson, The Idler, No. 11 (24 June 1758)

items.text: The happiest conversation is that of which nothing is distinctly remembered but a general effect of pleasing impression
items.context: Johnson's reflective observation on the nature of truly satisfying social interaction
items.publicReaction: The statement became recognized as expressing Johnson's understanding of conversation's value beyond specific memorable content
items.source: James Boswell, The Life of Samuel Johnson, LL.D. (London, 1791)

items.text: It is scarcely possible to pass an hour in honest conversation, without being able...to please ourselves with having given or received some advantages
items.context: From Rambler No. 80, Johnson's reflection on the mutual benefits of genuine conversation
items.publicReaction: The essay contributed to eighteenth-century valorization of social intercourse as morally and intellectually beneficial
items.source: Samuel Johnson, The Rambler, No. 80 (22 December 1750)

items.text: No style of conversation is more extensively acceptable than the narrative
items.context: From Rambler No. 188, Johnson's analysis of effective conversational styles
items.publicReaction: The observation influenced understanding of rhetoric and oral tradition in the eighteenth century
items.source: Samuel Johnson, The Rambler, No. 188 (4 January 1752)
